data.epi_content:
"Someone's knocking on the door again.

It must be Evan.

He said he cast some unnamed spell that would alert him immediately if anyone other than me entered the room or if any spells were cast.

I dragged my slightly tired feet and opened the front door.

"About three people came in, but I don't see them in the room."

"I put them in the bathtub. I was about to call you because of the smell, but you came at just the right time."

"When you say it like that, you make me sound like a crow."

"Anyway, since you're here, want to try smoking this?"

I took the slightly bitter tobacco I'd recently bought out of my mouth and handed it to Evan.

He seemed to grimace a bit, saying tobacco smelled bad, but since I'd had the same thought before I started smoking, I encouraged him strongly.

Here, if you develop lung cancer, you could just open up your chest, cut out the lungs, and use healing magic, so it wouldn't be a big problem.

It would hurt, but you'd have to endure it.

Evan took the tobacco I handed him with practiced ease, inhaled once, and looked slightly surprised.

Of course, it was heavily infused with chocolate flavor.

"How did you make this?"

"By putting chocolate underneath and coating it with the smoke."

"I wish you'd given me this the first time."

"Unfortunately, I didn't have the luxury to think of that."

I took out a small box from my chest, pulled out another cigarette, put it between my lips, and lit it.

Evan walked into the bathroom and began to decompose the bodies, raising something around them that he called blue but looked black to my eyes.

Literally like in that vulgar movie where a treasure hunter posing as an adventurer gets covered in ants that instantly reduce a person to remains and then carry away the pieces.

"Where does all that go when you decompose it?"

By now, I don't even call it a corpse or refer to it by its exact name.

After all, you can't call something that's been finely ground into unrecognizable powder by the name of what was once a person.

And even if I knew the name—for example, if it was the cursed name Lydia—I wouldn't particularly want to say it.

"Into the stomach of something demon-like, as the church would call it.

Unlike the noble God, these friends always give something in return for what I give them, which is nice."

The gods worshipped in the Empire seemed so good that they demanded goodness from others as well.

Especially from those who give.

"True, if something goes, something should come back."

But like most proverbs, pleasant and meaningful words often favored the strong.

All people in the world are equal under the god who created this world, but someone blessed with just a tiny bit of divine favor is more equal than others.

I've tried to erase this sense of inferiority, but if effort could solve everything in the world, even grand issues like world peace would have been easily resolved.

It's a sticky feeling that makes me feel like a base human while simultaneously making me feel alive, so for now, I'm just accepting it.
data.epi_content2:
Lydia and the two nameless girls had completely disappeared from the world, leaving only the faint scent of blood lingering in the room.

Even this would disappear with a brief ventilation and the aroma of tea that would follow, meaning their lives amounted to just that much.

Thinking this, I inhale the tobacco.

The stinging smoke pricks my lungs and awakens my mind.

At the same time, I feel slightly dizzy, and I notice the long cigarette has already burned down to gray ash that's warming my lips.

I didn't bother to tap it off.

I just pushed out my upper lip slightly and sighed, and it all flew away.

"Hey, Evan. What are you going to do from now on?"

"Suddenly talking about such vague things."

Evan stretched his words slightly at the end, making a curious expression as if asking me to explain.

"I just want no one to bother me.

Now that Lydia is dead, another child will probably attach themselves to me and start tormenting me.

I'm a pushover, and someone wants me dead.

That's enough for me. I'd like to just smoke tobacco in my room and die when my lungs rot."

"Well, hoping that others won't drag you out just because you want to stay in your room seems a bit too optimistic."

Evan continued, saying he felt the same way.

Of course, not many people, but important figures are interested in me.

More precisely, they don't care or even glance at me when I'm withering away alone in my room, but when I try to do something, when I try to move forward like a protagonist in a heroic tale, overcoming adversity, there are plenty of great individuals who don't just trip me but cut off my ankles.

Rather than explaining at length, I just give a light response that's meaningless but keeps the conversation going, like a bald monk looking out the window and chanting "mountains are mountains, waters are waters."

"It's good to dream big."

Anyone listening might think I'm saying something hopeful and bright.

"Dreams, huh? I can make them come true for you."

Whenever we have conversations like this, I feel the hair on my arms stand up.

Even though I don't have anything but white down on my arms.

It's like someone rubbed a balloon about to burst all over my arms.

"Your desire to live normally without worries isn't an easy thing to achieve.

And it's probably not easy for a farmer living in the countryside either."

With the final remark that not many people live without experiencing difficulties or dangers, Evan completely eliminated what was in the bathroom.

In the physical sense.

"Why."

"The Emperor, no, His Highness the Crown Prince said you're just someone who needs to be disposed of.

He said that while laughing and cheerfully talking about the things he did to you, so I let him enjoy the country's affectionate fireworks with his whole body."

Well, it's natural for a sane person to feel awkward having such a normal conversation after killing someone.

Even if you speak calmly, being able to talk about harming others without feeling anything is quite difficult.

But if my life is at stake, others' lives should naturally be disregarded.
data.epi_content3:
From the moment I decided to live, I decided to view those who wouldn't help me as insignificant as flies.

For example, to strengthen my resolve, I didn't torture Lydia to death but disposed of her lightly, like killing a mosquito that entered the room.

Just as blood gets on your hand when you catch a blood-sucking mosquito with your bare hand, blood splattered on my face due to the difference in size.

"By the way, I don't understand why someone attending the Academy has such poor magical quality."

"Ah, I can at least conjure a simple fire from my fingertips, but that friend was a talentless one who couldn't even do that."

Still, she had developed the virtues of a woman born into a noble family, that is, enough language skills and breasts to not be embarrassing when sent to a son of another family.

But for what was once Lydia, now reduced to powder, the Count and his wife could no longer think of any use for her other than fertilizer to be scattered on soil.

"Anyway, I've sacrificed about 30 people so far, so you don't even need to think about dying in a fight here.

Erica, what you want is just a life where no one bothers you and leaves you alone, right?"

I nodded.

"It's quite explicit so I haven't said it directly until now, but you are roughly..."

Evan frowns and starts counting on his fingers.

After multiplying and adding a few times, he seems to finish calculating and holds up one finger.

"One of you. And just ordinary people living normally, neither evil nor particularly good. Therefore, utterly ordinary people—a million of them.

Which do you think is more important?"

I answer without hesitation.

If I were going to hesitate, I would have fled from Evan long ago.

The Evan before me is not the boy who loved books, magic, and had many dreams.

Not the Evan who loved Vivian, but a crazy murderer, or a cursed black magician Evan.

"Obviously me."

At those words, Evan shrugs as if he knew I would say that, looking at me as if seeing someone of his kind.

But I had a trace of self-reproach, or rather a base and vile conscience that I wasn't killing directly, so I deliberately ignored it and moved on.

"Well, I'm not saying we should go out and cause trouble right now.

If we did, His Majesty sitting on that high castle would find it uncomfortable and have my head mounted on a window ledge."

"I don't quite understand what you're saying."

At that, Evan walks around the room, wiggling his legs as if feeling like a teacher.

Then, with a slightly smug face, he said to me:

"It's not a marriage proposal, but after watching for the right time, let's go live somewhere suitable.

We can't stay cooped up in this narrow place forever.

And having the disposal space attached to the sleeping space is quite unsettling.

I'm handling it well now so nothing will come out, but if I mess up, they might emerge as vengeful spirits and try to kill you."

I understood roughly what he meant by watching for the right time, so instead of questioning, I just slightly raised the corners of my mouth and nodded.

If they hadn't hated me so much, I wouldn't have hated them either.

Conversation is already meaningless.

I put the tobacco in my mouth and lit it.
